slug: 你的猫看你的方式和看一只大猫一样
title: 你的猫看你的方式和看一只大猫一样

outline.concept: Cats don't have a separate social category for 'human.' Behaviorally, they process you as a large, non-hostile conspecific—another cat, just bigger and weirdly shaped. They deploy the same social grammar toward you that they'd use with another cat, with no specialized human-directed signals.
outline.query: 猫 盯我 眼神 当成 同类 大猫

outline.concept: Adult cats almost never meow at other adult cats—meowing is a kitten-to-mother solicitation call. When your grown cat meows at you, it's displaying neotenic (juvenile-retained) behavior: treating you as a maternal figure, the same way a kitten would beg its mother for care.
outline.query: 猫 对我叫 喵 成年猫 小猫叫 撒娇

outline.concept: Slow blinking, head bunting (rubbing scent glands on you), and allogrooming are cat-to-cat affiliative signals with specific social functions. Your cat isn't simplifying these for you—it's running the same full social protocol it would with a trusted fellow cat.
outline.query: 猫 慢眨眼 眯眼 头蹭 舔我 亲近

outline.concept: Dogs co-evolved with humans for ~30,000 years and developed dedicated human-reading abilities (comprehending pointing, using eye contact as bonding). Cats domesticated themselves ~10,000 years ago with zero selection for human-specific communication—they simply mapped their existing feline social system onto us.
outline.query: 狗 懂手势 指方向 眼神交流 猫 不理人 对比

outline.concept: Your cat's affection isn't 'less real' because it's cat-cat signaling—it's the genuine article. A slow blink means 'I trust you and feel safe' in cat, and your cat means it identically whether blinking at you or another cat. The signal didn't dilute; the recipient just changed.
outline.query: 猫 信任我 依赖 真感情 喜欢 真心
